Costa Rica Histórica

Obras Públicas

Construcción de caminos, pistas y vías fluviales

A small documentary set spanning three threads of Costa Rican infrastructure history: the grading of an airstrip at Liberia, the cutting of the Interamerican Highway through Guanacaste's hill country, and river transport on the Río Tempisque before the age of bridges and paved roads.

Plate Group I

LIR — La Pista de Liberia

Construction of the Liberia airstrip, Guanacaste — drainage works and runway grading through dry thorn-scrub terrain.

LIR-01
Real Photo — Obra Vial

Tubería de drenaje junto a la pista

Large-diameter concrete or asbestos-cement culvert sections laid in a row beside a drainage channel, with reserve stock piled near the graded surface beyond. Drainage infrastructure of this kind was essential groundwork before grading could proceed reliably through Guanacaste's seasonal flood plains — the wet-season runoff that cuts the visible channel here would otherwise undermine any unprotected fill.

Guanacaste Drenaje Infraestructura aérea
LIR-02
Real Photo — Obra Vial

Eje de la pista con estacas de agrimensura

A freshly bladed strip stretches toward the horizon, its centerline marked by a row of wooden survey stakes — the working line a grading crew follows before any surface treatment is applied. A small cairn of stones in the foreground likely marks a benchmark or station point left by the surveying team.

Agrimensura Pista en obra
LIR-03
Real Photo — Obra Vial

"LIR — The runway under construction"

LIR — The runway under construction

The captioned plate of this small set, confirming the site as Liberia's airfield by its IATA code. The grading here is further advanced than in LIR-02 — a smoother, more compacted surface with centerline markers still visible, framed by low hills and scrub typical of the dry Pacific lowlands of Guanacaste.

LIR Captioned plate
Plate Group II

La Interamericana en Obra

Construction of the Inter-American Highway — heavy equipment, cuts and fills through hill country, and a finished gravel grade.

CA-01
Real Photo — Obra Vial

Maquinaria pesada abriendo el corte

A Caterpillar-branded tractor-scraper and a tracked bulldozer work a fresh cut through forested hill country, raw graded surface visible between them and spoil piled along both verges. The scale of earthmoving needed to push the highway through this terrain — likely a stretch of the more mountainous Pacific-slope or Valley Central section — is evident in the depth of the cut against the tree line.

Caterpillar Corte y relleno
CA-02
Real Photo — Obra Vial

Camino terminado entre palmeras

A long, straight graded roadbed runs through a valley dotted with royal palms, the surface compacted and finished — likely an earlier-completed stretch of the same project, or an access road built in connection with it. The avenue of palms along the left bank suggests proximity to a settled finca or hacienda.

Tramo terminado Valle
CA-03
Real Photo — Obra Vial

"Interamerican Highway Under Construction"

Interamerican Highway Under Construction

The captioned plate confirming the project. A graded, partly surfaced two-lane roadbed runs past deep cut embankments and a fence line of living posts, with a bulldozer working further up the grade. Built largely through the 1940s–50s with U.S. Bureau of Public Roads cooperation, this corridor became Costa Rica's central artery for the Inter-American Highway system.

Captioned plate Interamericana
Plate Group III

Río Tempisque

River transport on the Tempisque before bridges and paved roads reached Guanacaste.

TQ-01
Real Photo — Vía Fluvial

Orilla del Tempisque

A bend of the river framed by gallery forest on both banks. Cattle stand at the water's edge on the left, tended by a figure barely visible among the trees, while a large bleached driftwood snag rests in the shallows of the foreground — a quiet, undeveloped stretch of the river, likely not far from the working landing shown in the companion plate.

Guanacaste Bosque de galería Ganado
TQ-02
Real Photo — Vía Fluvial

"Tempisque"

Tempisque

A covered river launch moored at the bank, several passengers and crew visible on deck, with small rowboats tied alongside and an open timber-frame structure rising aft — possibly a cargo gantry or paddlewheel housing. Before the Inter-American Highway and the later Taiwan-funded bridge reached the peninsula, vessels like this carried passengers, cattle, and goods along the Tempisque, Guanacaste's principal navigable river.

Captioned plate Lancha fluvial
Costa Rica Histórica — Colección Craig
